examples.title: Compile-Time Polymorphism - Function Overloading
examples.description: Demonstrates function overloading where multiple functions have the same name but different parameter types.
examples.explanation: Function overloading allows multiple functions with the same name but different parameters. The compiler decides which function to call based on the argument types at compile time. This is also known as compile-time polymorphism or static polymorphism.

examples.title: Runtime Polymorphism - Virtual Functions
examples.description: Demonstrates runtime polymorphism using virtual functions and base class pointers.
examples.explanation: Runtime polymorphism is achieved using virtual functions. When a function is declared as virtual in the base class, the appropriate derived class function is called based on the actual object type, not the pointer type. The 'override' keyword ensures the derived class function correctly overrides the base class virtual function.

examples.title: Pure Virtual Functions & Abstract Base Class
examples.description: Demonstrates abstract classes with pure virtual functions creating an interface for derived classes.
examples.explanation: An abstract class contains pure virtual functions (marked with '= 0') that have no implementation. It serves as an interface that derived classes must implement. This demonstrates runtime polymorphism where different shapes (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle) share a common interface but provide their own implementations for area() and perimeter() calculations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of shapes
